visuals.title: Basic and Extended Regular Expressions
visuals.description: Animated visual comparing BRE and ERE metacharacters, character classes, tool usage, and practical examples in Unix

animations.narration: Regular Expressions are powerful pattern-matching tools in Unix, divided into Basic (BRE) and Extended (ERE) types.

Basic Regular Expression metacharacters include dot for any character, asterisk for zero or more, caret for start of line, dollar for end, brackets for character class, and backslash-parentheses for grouping.
Extended Regular Expressions add plus for one or more, question mark for zero or one, pipe for alternation, parentheses without backslash for grouping, and curly braces for quantifiers.
Character classes let you match specific sets: a-z for lowercase, 0-9 for digits, caret-abc to negate, and POSIX classes like alpha, digit, and space for portability.
BRE is used by grep and sed, requiring backslashes for grouping and quantifiers. ERE is used by egrep and awk, where plus, question mark, pipe, and parentheses work without backslashes.
Practical examples: use grep with caret to find lines starting with Error, sed with s-command for substitution, egrep to match email patterns, and egrep to match IP address patterns.
